news_summary: Pacific Island nations have pioneered regional guidance on climate relocation, while Mexico advances digital rights by partially unblocking secure internet access.
news_summary_fr: Les nations insulaires du Pacifique ont été les premières à adopter des directives régionales en matière de relocalisation climatique, tandis que le Mexique fait progresser les droits numériques en débloquant partiellement l'accès sécurisé à Internet.
news_summary_es: Las naciones insulares del Pacífico han sido pioneras en la orientación regional sobre la reubicación climática, mientras que México promueve los derechos digitales al desbloquear parcialmente el acceso seguro a Internet.

articles.title: Pacific Islands: World’s First Regional Guidance on Climate Relocation
articles.summary:
Click to expand Image
          



  
        
  

        
                      
      
        
              Boy on a boat in the mangroves near the village of Walande, Malaita Province, Solomon Islands.
                    © 2025 Cyril Eberle for Human Rights Watch
          
    


  
Pacific Island governments on March 4, 2026, launched the world’s first regional guide to address climate-related relocation grounded in human rights principles. As Pacific leaders gather for a three-day convening in Nadi, Fiji, they have an opportunity to pair this landmark guidance with the financial and technical support needed to implement it. The Pacific Regional Guidance on Planned Relocation adapts global standards on planned relocation to the realities of the Pacific, where rising seas, coastal erosion, and king tides are already forcing entire communities to move. “This regional guide recognizes that planned relocation to a new site is a measure of last resort for communities that can no longer adapt to climate change impacts in place, and should protect communities’ rights, dignity, and self-determination,” said Erica Bower, climate displacement researcher at Human Rights Watch. “Now, Pacific governments and donors need to ensure communities have the resources to make those principles real.”The guidance covers key considerations to support planned relocation in the Pacific, including robust assessments, policy frameworks, funding, and mechanisms for communities to have a seat at the table during all stages of decision-making. It tailors global standards on planned relocation to the complexities of the Pacific, including greater focus on the role of customary land tenure systems, cultural heritage in assessments, and Indigenous rights such as self-determination and free, prior, and informed consent to make changes in their living situation.In March 2025, Human Rights Watch published “There’s Just No More Land,” a report documenting the experience of Walande, an Indigenous community in Solomon Islands that planned a collective relocation to mainland Malaita after repeated flooding made their island homes uninhabitable. Walande’s relocation, largely community-led and self-financed through remittances, demonstrates both the resilience of affected communities and the risks they continue to face when government support is limited.Walande’s experience underscores what is at stake across the Pacific. Without adequate government and international support and sustained funding, relocation can lead to new challenges, including continuous flood exposure, food insecurity, and conflicts with host communities over limited land. The guidance recognizes that international law protects a range of rights implicated in climate-related planned relocations, including the rights to adequate housing, food, water, health, and culture. Countries have obligations to respect, protect, and fulfill these rights without discrimination. Regional and global cooperation can help ensure that island states facing disproportionate climate risks are not left to shoulder these responsibilities alone.Governments should also ensure that national frameworks on planned relocation, including Solomon Islands’ forthcoming Standard Operating Procedures and Vanuatu’s forthcoming guidelines, align with the regional guidance and provide clear pathways for community leadership and direct access to funding.But principles alone will not protect communities, Human Rights Watch said. Governments and international partners should mobilize sustained financial and technical resources, including through mechanisms such as the Pacific Resilience Facility, a new Pacific-led grant-making entity, to ensure communities can access land, housing, infrastructure, and livelihood support.If fully implemented, the Pacific Guidance could serve as a model for other regions facing the sobering realities of planned relocation. Governments in the Caribbean, another region highly exposed to sea level rise, could host similar convenings for exchange and could develop similar regional guidance tailored to the needs of their region. “The Pacific has shown global leadership by agreeing on a rights-respecting regional approach to planned relocation,” Bower said. “Now, governments in the region and supporters around the world should ensure communities like Walande have the resources and long-term support to adapt with dignity and shape their own futures.”
articles.content:
Click to expand Image Boy on a boat in the mangroves near the village of Walande, Malaita Province, Solomon Islands. © 2025 Cyril Eberle for Human Rights Watch

Pacific Island governments on March 4, 2026, launched the world’s first regional guide to address climate-related relocation grounded in human rights principles.

As Pacific leaders gather for a three-day convening in Nadi, Fiji, they have an opportunity to pair this landmark guidance with the financial and technical support needed to implement it. The Pacific Regional Guidance on Planned Relocation adapts global standards on planned relocation to the realities of the Pacific, where rising seas, coastal erosion, and king tides are already forcing entire communities to move.

“This regional guide recognizes that planned relocation to a new site is a measure of last resort for communities that can no longer adapt to climate change impacts in place, and should protect communities’ rights, dignity, and self-determination,” said Erica Bower, climate displacement researcher at Human Rights Watch. “Now, Pacific governments and donors need to ensure communities have the resources to make those principles real.”

The guidance covers key considerations to support planned relocation in the Pacific, including robust assessments, policy frameworks, funding, and mechanisms for communities to have a seat at the table during all stages of decision-making. It tailors global standards on planned relocation to the complexities of the Pacific, including greater focus on the role of customary land tenure systems, cultural heritage in assessments, and Indigenous rights such as self-determination and free, prior, and informed consent to make changes in their living situation.

In March 2025, Human Rights Watch published “There’s Just No More Land,” a report documenting the experience of Walande, an Indigenous community in Solomon Islands that planned a collective relocation to mainland Malaita after repeated flooding made their island homes uninhabitable. Walande’s relocation, largely community-led and self-financed through remittances, demonstrates both the resilience of affected communities and the risks they continue to face when government support is limited.

Walande’s experience underscores what is at stake across the Pacific. Without adequate government and international support and sustained funding, relocation can lead to new challenges, including continuous flood exposure, food insecurity, and conflicts with host communities over limited land.

The guidance recognizes that international law protects a range of rights implicated in climate-related planned relocations, including the rights to adequate housing, food, water, health, and culture. Countries have obligations to respect, protect, and fulfill these rights without discrimination. Regional and global cooperation can help ensure that island states facing disproportionate climate risks are not left to shoulder these responsibilities alone.

Governments should also ensure that national frameworks on planned relocation, including Solomon Islands’ forthcoming Standard Operating Procedures and Vanuatu’s forthcoming guidelines, align with the regional guidance and provide clear pathways for community leadership and direct access to funding.

But principles alone will not protect communities, Human Rights Watch said. Governments and international partners should mobilize sustained financial and technical resources, including through mechanisms such as the Pacific Resilience Facility, a new Pacific-led grant-making entity, to ensure communities can access land, housing, infrastructure, and livelihood support.

If fully implemented, the Pacific Guidance could serve as a model for other regions facing the sobering realities of planned relocation. Governments in the Caribbean, another region highly exposed to sea level rise, could host similar convenings for exchange and could develop similar regional guidance tailored to the needs of their region.

“The Pacific has shown global leadership by agreeing on a rights-respecting regional approach to planned relocation,” Bower said. “Now, governments in the region and supporters around the world should ensure communities like Walande have the resources and long-term support to adapt with dignity and shape their own futures.”
articles.reasoning: The article reports the launch of the world’s first regional guidance on climate-related relocation grounded in human rights principles, addressing a critical and growing global challenge. It highlights a significant, tangible policy advancement with broad implications for Pacific Island communities facing climate displacement, emphasizing community rights, dignity, and self-determination. The guidance includes detailed frameworks for implementation, funding, and community participation, and it sets a precedent that could inspire other vulnerable regions, demonstrating substantial positive real-world impact and meaningful scope.
articles.personality_title: Pacific Islands launch first regional guide for climate-related relocation
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context**
Pacific Island communities are facing serious problems from climate change. Rising seas, strong tides, and coastal erosion are making some islands unsafe to live on. This means whole communities need to move to new places, which is a difficult and sensitive process.

**What happened**
On March 4, 2026, Pacific Island governments introduced the world’s first regional guide to help communities move safely because of climate change. This guide, called the Pacific Regional Guidance on Planned Relocation, was created during a meeting in Nadi, Fiji. It is based on human rights and focuses on protecting the rights, culture, and wishes of the people who have to move.

**Impact**
This guide is important because it is the first time a whole region has agreed on clear rules for moving communities affected by climate change. It includes ways to involve communities in decisions, respects Indigenous rights, and considers local land traditions. The guide also highlights the need for government and international support to help communities avoid problems like food shortages or conflicts over land.

**What’s next step**
Governments in the Pacific will work on national plans that match this regional guide. They will also try to get money and technical help through new programs like the Pacific Resilience Facility. If this guide is fully used, it could become an example for other areas, like the Caribbean, that face similar climate risks.

**One-sentence takeaway**
Pacific Island nations have created the first regional plan to support communities forced to move by climate change, focusing on human rights and local leadership.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les îles du Pacifique lancent le premier guide régional pour la relocation liée au climat
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte**
Les communautés des îles du Pacifique sont confrontées à de graves problèmes causés par le changement climatique. La montée des mers, les fortes marées et l’érosion côtière rendent certaines îles dangereuses pour y vivre. Cela oblige des communautés entières à déménager, ce qui est un processus difficile et sensible.

**Ce qui s’est passé**
Le 4 mars 2026, les gouvernements des îles du Pacifique ont présenté le premier guide régional au monde pour aider les communautés à déménager en raison du changement climatique. Ce guide, appelé Guide régional du Pacifique sur la relocation planifiée, a été créé lors d’une réunion à Nadi, aux Fidji. Il est basé sur les droits humains et vise à protéger les droits, la culture et les souhaits des personnes concernées.

**Impact**
Ce guide est important car c’est la première fois qu’une région entière adopte des règles claires pour déplacer des communautés touchées par le climat. Il inclut des moyens pour que les communautés participent aux décisions, respecte les droits des peuples autochtones et prend en compte les traditions foncières locales. Le guide souligne aussi la nécessité d’un soutien gouvernemental et international pour éviter des problèmes comme la pénurie alimentaire ou les conflits fonciers.

**Prochaine étape**
Les gouvernements du Pacifique vont élaborer des plans nationaux conformes à ce guide régional. Ils chercheront aussi à obtenir des financements et une aide technique via de nouveaux programmes comme le Pacific Resilience Facility. Si le guide est pleinement appliqué, il pourrait servir d’exemple pour d’autres régions, comme les Caraïbes, qui font face à des risques climatiques similaires.

**Résumé en une phrase**
Les nations insulaires du Pacifique ont créé le premier plan régional pour soutenir les communautés forcées de déménager à cause du changement climatique, en mettant l’accent sur les droits humains et le leadership local.
articles.personality_title_es: Islas del Pacífico lanzan la primera guía regional para reubicación climática
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto**
Las comunidades de las islas del Pacífico enfrentan graves problemas por el cambio climático. El aumento del nivel del mar, las mareas fuertes y la erosión costera hacen que algunas islas sean peligrosas para vivir. Esto obliga a comunidades enteras a mudarse, lo que es un proceso difícil y delicado.

**Qué pasó**
El 4 de marzo de 2026, los gobiernos de las islas del Pacífico presentaron la primera guía regional del mundo para ayudar a las comunidades a mudarse debido al cambio climático. Esta guía, llamada Guía Regional del Pacífico sobre Reubicación Planificada, se creó durante una reunión en Nadi, Fiji. Se basa en los derechos humanos y busca proteger los derechos, la cultura y los deseos de las personas afectadas.

**Impacto**
Esta guía es importante porque es la primera vez que toda una región acuerda reglas claras para mover a comunidades afectadas por el clima. Incluye formas de involucrar a las comunidades en las decisiones, respeta los derechos indígenas y considera las tradiciones locales de la tierra. La guía también destaca la necesidad de apoyo gubernamental e internacional para evitar problemas como la inseguridad alimentaria o conflictos por la tierra.

**Próximo paso**
Los gobiernos del Pacífico trabajarán en planes nacionales que coincidan con esta guía regional. También buscarán obtener fondos y ayuda técnica a través de nuevos programas como el Pacific Resilience Facility. Si se usa completamente, esta guía podría ser un ejemplo para otras regiones, como el Caribe, que enfrentan riesgos climáticos similares.

**Resumen en una frase**
Las naciones insulares del Pacífico crearon el primer plan regional para apoyar a las comunidades forzadas a mudarse por el cambio climático, centrado en los derechos humanos y el liderazgo local.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a small wooden boat gently floating through dense mangrove roots near a peaceful island shoreline, with soft silhouettes of traditional Pacific island homes in the background and calm waters reflecting a rising tide, symbolizing community resilience and the journey of planned relocation amidst climate challenges.

articles.title: Mexican government partially unblocks secure internet
articles.summary: The Tor network is used by millions of users daily to securely access the internet, safeguard their right to information and freedom of expression, and avoid censorship or government surveillance.
articles.content:
By Jacobo Nájera and Miguel Trujillo

This article was originally published on The Tor Project on November 17, 2025. An edited version is republished on Global Voices under a media partnership agreement.

Across at least the last two six-year presidential terms in Mexico, and through the first year of the current administration, access to the federal government's primary websites for information and public services over the Tor network was blocked.

A study by the authors of this article published October 9, 2023, reports that 21 government agencies blocked access from the Tor network. The blockage was partially lifted in the first days of July 2025.

The Tor network is used by millions of users daily to securely access the internet, safeguard their right to information and free speech, and evade censorship or government surveillance. In Mexico, an estimated 20 thousand people use it, according to data published by the Tor Project.

In Mexico, Tor has been adopted by civil society organizations and collectives, including the implementation of anonymous mailboxes in journalistic outlets and digital safeguards in human rights advocacies.

Under the previous administration, the Mexican government acknowledged the benefits of using Tor for the purposes of combating corruption. It established a mailbox for the Ministry of the Civil Service using Tor. Thus, the government established a regulatory framework which acknowledges the need to guarantee anonymity for whistleblower activities within the civil service. A notice published in the Official Gazette of the Federation stated:

Safeguarding confidentiality: […] is an obligation for all persons assigned to the Bureau[;] the Coordinator General, assisted by the Coordinator, shall supervise, control, and evaluate all activities of the System [to ensure that] they guarantee anonymity of whistleblowers and information, taking the measures they deem necessary, and shall implement actions for improvement in furtherance of such efforts.

The administration of [former] President Enrique Pena Nieto (2012-2018) did not acknowledge the blockage, whereas under Andres Manuel Lopez Obrador (2018–2024) the government acknowledged that it maintained the blockage and explained that it did so for security reasons.

(…) protective measures are taken, such as blocking network traffic considered malicious, automated, or potentially threatening, which affects users of the Tor network, since its very nature makes it impossible to distinguish between ordinary and automated users, which is considered a risk to gob.mx, for which reason access over that network is restricted.

Juan Carlos Guerrero Torres, Director of Legal Analysis and Document Management in the president's office, which is responsible for digital strategy and the development of lines and operation of digital infrastructure, replied to Global Voices.

Regarding this response, the Tor Project, through its spokesman Pavel Zoneff, informed us that there are workarounds which show that it is possible to achieve a balance between minimizing risks for both users and websites without denying internet access to parties that depend on the Tor network. In his words:

Blocking entire sections of the internet based on the outdated belief that all traffic from the Tor network is indistinguishable or malicious is a mistake and puts people at risk. There are many ways for websites to defend against threats such as DoS attacks, bots, and other malicious actions without isolating a significant part of the global online community.

Current administration reverses policy

On the other hand, on May 6, 2025, the current administration under President Claudia Sheinbaum Pardo declared that it has no policy position or documented justification to block access by Tor users to the website www.gob.mx. As (sic) stated by Gabriela Ignacio Vazquez, Coordinator of Digital Infrastructure and Liaison for Transparency of the National Bureau of Digital Infrastructure in the Digital Transformation Agency, responsible for orienting the relevant policy.

On July 5, in the course of routine monitoring for research purposes, we confirmed that the block on www.gob.mx had been lifted. Thus, it is now possible to access the Mexican government's primary information site -and by extension information from the different government agencies concentrated there- from the Tor network.

However, we also found that datos.gob.mx maintains the blockage, and the whistleblower mailbox, which previously admitted support for Tor users, was disabled and replaced with the SIDEC platform, which does not permit access from the Tor network.
articles.reasoning: The Mexican government's partial lifting of the Tor network blockage restores secure, anonymous access to public information and supports freedom of expression and the right to information for thousands of users. This policy reversal represents a meaningful step toward digital rights and transparency, benefiting civil society and human rights advocates broadly.
articles.personality_title: Mexico partially lifts block on Tor network, restoring access to government information
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – For several years, Mexico blocked access to its main government websites through the Tor network, a tool that helps people browse the internet securely and anonymously. This block affected about 20,000 users in Mexico, including journalists and human rights groups who rely on Tor to protect their privacy.

**What happened** – In early July 2025, Mexico’s government lifted the block on its main website, www.gob.mx, allowing Tor users to access public information again. This change came after the current administration under President Claudia Sheinbaum Pardo said it had no official reason to keep blocking Tor users. However, some sites like datos.gob.mx remain blocked, and the whistleblower mailbox that worked with Tor was replaced by a platform that does not allow Tor access.

**Impact** – Restoring access to the government’s main site through Tor is important because it helps people safely seek information without fear of being watched or censored. This is especially significant for activists and journalists who need privacy to do their work. The decision shows a move toward respecting digital rights and transparency in Mexico.

**What's next step** – The government might continue to review and possibly remove other blocks on Tor, including restoring access to platforms like the whistleblower mailbox. Advocates hope this leads to broader acceptance of secure internet tools that protect users’ privacy.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Mexico’s partial removal of the Tor network block opens safer access to government information, supporting privacy and free expression for thousands of users.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le Mexique lève partiellement le blocage du réseau Tor, rétablissant l'accès aux sites gouvernementaux
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Pendant plusieurs années, le Mexique a bloqué l'accès à ses principaux sites gouvernementaux via le réseau Tor, un outil qui permet aux utilisateurs de naviguer sur Internet de manière sécurisée et anonyme. Ce blocage affectait environ 20 000 utilisateurs au Mexique, notamment des journalistes et des groupes de défense des droits humains qui dépendent de Tor pour protéger leur vie privée.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Début juillet 2025, le gouvernement mexicain a levé le blocage de son site principal, www.gob.mx, permettant aux utilisateurs de Tor d'accéder à nouveau aux informations publiques. Ce changement est intervenu après que l'administration actuelle, dirigée par la présidente Claudia Sheinbaum Pardo, a déclaré ne pas avoir de raison officielle de continuer à bloquer les utilisateurs de Tor. Cependant, certains sites comme datos.gob.mx restent bloqués, et la boîte aux lettres pour lanceurs d'alerte compatible avec Tor a été remplacée par une plateforme qui n'autorise pas l'accès via Tor.

**Impact** – Rétablir l'accès au site principal du gouvernement via Tor est important car cela aide les gens à chercher des informations en toute sécurité, sans craindre d'être surveillés ou censurés. C'est particulièrement crucial pour les militants et les journalistes qui ont besoin de confidentialité pour leur travail. Cette décision marque une avancée vers le respect des droits numériques et de la transparence au Mexique.

**Prochaine étape** – Le gouvernement pourrait continuer à revoir et éventuellement supprimer d'autres blocages sur Tor, y compris le rétablissement de l'accès à des plateformes comme la boîte aux lettres pour lanceurs d'alerte. Les défenseurs espèrent que cela conduira à une acceptation plus large des outils Internet sécurisés qui protègent la vie privée des utilisateurs.

**Résumé en une phrase** – La levée partielle du blocage du réseau Tor au Mexique permet un accès plus sûr aux informations gouvernementales, soutenant la vie privée et la liberté d'expression pour des milliers d'utilisateurs.
articles.personality_title_es: México levanta parcialmente el bloqueo de la red Tor, restaurando acceso a sitios gubernamentales
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Durante varios años, México bloqueó el acceso a sus principales sitios web gubernamentales a través de la red Tor, una herramienta que ayuda a las personas a navegar por internet de forma segura y anónima. Este bloqueo afectó a unas 20,000 personas en México, incluidos periodistas y grupos de derechos humanos que dependen de Tor para proteger su privacidad.

**Qué pasó** – A principios de julio de 2025, el gobierno mexicano levantó el bloqueo en su sitio principal, www.gob.mx, permitiendo que los usuarios de Tor accedieran nuevamente a la información pública. Este cambio ocurrió después de que la administración actual, bajo la presidencia de Claudia Sheinbaum Pardo, declaró que no tenía una razón oficial para seguir bloqueando a los usuarios de Tor. Sin embargo, algunos sitios como datos.gob.mx siguen bloqueados, y el buzón para denunciantes que funcionaba con Tor fue reemplazado por una plataforma que no permite acceso desde Tor.

**Impacto** – Restaurar el acceso al sitio principal del gobierno a través de Tor es importante porque ayuda a las personas a buscar información de manera segura, sin temor a ser vigilados o censurados. Esto es especialmente significativo para activistas y periodistas que necesitan privacidad para su trabajo. La decisión muestra un avance hacia el respeto de los derechos digitales y la transparencia en México.

**Próximo paso** – El gobierno podría continuar revisando y posiblemente eliminando otros bloqueos en Tor, incluyendo restaurar el acceso a plataformas como el buzón para denunciantes. Los defensores esperan que esto conduzca a una mayor aceptación de herramientas de internet seguras que protegen la privacidad de los usuarios.

**Resumen en una frase** – La eliminación parcial del bloqueo a la red Tor en México permite un acceso más seguro a la información gubernamental, apoyando la privacidad y la libre expresión de miles de usuarios.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a sturdy, ancient key unlocking a glowing digital gateway shaped like a stylized Mexican government building, surrounded by softly illuminated, intertwined network cables gently lifting a semi-transparent dark veil symbolizing censorship, with subtle silhouettes of watchful eyes fading away in the background.
